2.20: A type: 'The man is a close relative of ours, one of our guardian-redeemers.' Boaz is the OT's most fully developed type of Christ as redeemer: (1) he is near of kin — Christ became our kinsman through the incarnation (Heb 2:14-15: he shared in flesh and blood that through death he might destroy him who has the power of death); (2) he has the right to redeem — as the sinless son of God, Christ alone qualifies; (3) he is willing to redeem — another go'el existed but declined (Ruth 4:6); Christ took on the obligation no one else could fulfill; (4) he pays the redemption price — Boaz redeems the land and takes Ruth; Christ redeems his people by his blood and takes them as his bride (Eph 5:25-27; Rev 19:7-9). The kinsman-redeemer motif is the book of Ruth's entire Christological contribution.
2.1: A theme: Naomi has a kinsman (mōdaʿ), a man of standing (gibbôr ḥayil), whose name is Boaz. The redeemer is introduced before Ruth knows he exists. God’s provision is in place before those who need it discover it. Christ was “the Lamb slain from the foundation of the world” (Rev 13:8) — the redemption was prepared before the fall that required it.
2.2: A theme: Ruth requests permission to glean — to live on the margins of another’s harvest, dependent on the owner’s generosity and the gleaning laws (Lev 19:9-10). Her humility in asking models the posture of those who approach Christ: coming to receive what they cannot produce, acknowledging dependence on another’s abundance. The one who has nothing comes to the one who has everything, and receives.
2.3: A revelation of God: “As it happened, she came to the part of the field belonging to Boaz.” The narrator flags divine providence operating through apparent coincidence. God’s hidden direction governs the seemingly random encounter — the same pattern as the Ethiopian eunuch on the Gaza road (Acts 8:26-27), the woman at the well (John 4:4: Jesus “had to pass through Samaria”), Zacchaeus in the sycamore tree (Luke 19:4-5).
2.4: A theme: Boaz greets workers with “The LORD be with you” — they respond in kind. The man of hesed names YHWH in ordinary speech; his workplace is sabbath-shaped. Christ brings the character of God into every human encounter: “full of grace and truth” (John 1:14). The blessing exchanged between Boaz and his workers models the community of the redeemed who bless one another in YHWH’s name.
2.5: A theme: Boaz asks “Whose young woman is this?” The redeemer inquires after the one who will become his own. This seeking posture mirrors Christ’s initiative: “The Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost” (Luke 19:10). The redeemer’s question precedes the redeemed’s petition.
2.6: A theme: the foreman identifies Ruth as “the Moabite.” Her foreign identity is stated first. Boaz knows she is an outsider before he speaks to her — and speaks anyway. Christ similarly chose to extend covenant belonging to those whose exclusion from it was legally established (Eph 2:12-13: those who were “without God in the world” are brought near through his blood).
2.7: A theme: Ruth asks to glean even among the sheaves — beyond what the law minimally guaranteed. She asks for more than her legal entitlement. Those who approach Christ with expectant petition receive beyond what the law can provide: “abundantly more than all we ask or think” (Eph 3:20). The posture of asking rather than assuming is met with excess.
2.8: A theme: Boaz addresses Ruth as “my daughter” — covenant kinship language extended to a stranger. The redeemer speaks belonging before the legal adoption is finalized. Christ addresses those still distant with the vocabulary of family (Matt 25:34: “Come, you who are blessed by my Father, inherit the kingdom”), calling them children before the full adoption is complete.
2.9: A theme: Boaz provides water before Ruth asks. “When you are thirsty, go to the vessels and drink from what the young men have drawn.” The redeemer anticipates thirst before it is voiced. Christ identifies himself as the answer to universal thirst: “If anyone thirsts, let him come to me and drink” (John 7:37); “whoever drinks of the water I give will never be thirsty again” (John 4:14). Every drink of water given to the weary points toward the living water.
2.10: A theme: Ruth falls with her face to the ground: “Why have I found favor in your eyes, since I am a foreigner?” The right response to unexpected grace is prostrate wonder and honest self-assessment. The centurion’s declaration — “Lord, I am not worthy to have you come under my roof” (Matt 8:8) — is the same posture. Those who recognize they have no claim on the redeemer’s grace are in the right position to receive it.
2.11: A revelation of God: Boaz has heard everything Ruth did for Naomi. The redeemer already knows the story of the one he is about to redeem. Christ’s knowledge of his own is complete before they approach him: “I know my own” (John 10:14); “Before I formed you in the womb I knew you” (Jer 1:5). The omniscience of the redeemer makes his grace more personal, not less.
2.12: A type: “May the LORD repay you... under whose wings you have come to take refuge.” Boaz prays that YHWH will give Ruth what she has sought — and in ch 3 he becomes the answer when Ruth asks him to spread his garment (kānāp, same word as “wing”) over her. The redeemer prays for what he will himself provide. Christ intercedes for his people (Heb 7:25; John 17) and is himself the shelter he prays they will find.
2.13: A theme: Ruth says she has been comforted and spoken to kindly, “though I am not one of your servants.” She has received belonging-language while her legal status is still “outsider.” This is the grammar of grace: treated as belonging before the adoption is legally complete. Paul captures the same paradox in Eph 2:19: those who were “strangers and aliens” are “now fellow citizens with the saints and members of the household of God.”
2.14: A shadow: Boaz invites Ruth to share bread and vinegar at mealtime with the reapers. Table fellowship extended to the outsider anticipates the eucharistic table where Christ invites those far off to eat and drink with him. The messianic banquet (Isa 25:6; Luke 14:15-24) reaches its institutional form in the Lord’s Supper; every anticipation of the redeemer sharing his table with the excluded is a foretaste.
2.15: A theme: Boaz grants Ruth gleaning access even among the sheaves — beyond the minimum the law requires — and tells the young men not to rebuke her. The redeemer gives not just provision but safety; not just access but welcome. Grace exceeds the request and adds protection. This pattern of excess governs Christ’s giving: “full grace upon grace” (John 1:16).
2.16: A theme: Boaz instructs his men to drop stalks deliberately for her — provision hidden within what appears to be natural gleaning. The dignifying grace that maintains the recipient’s agency rather than making charity obvious mirrors the God who gives in ways that honor those who receive: “do not let your left hand know what your right hand is doing” (Matt 6:3). Grace does not humiliate; it honors.
2.17: A theme: Ruth gleans an ephah of barley — an extraordinary amount by any gleaning standard, approximately a month’s supply for two people. The redeemer’s field yields beyond expectation. The twelve baskets of fragments after feeding five thousand (Matt 14:20), the hundredfold harvest (Mark 4:20), the wine abundance at Cana (John 2:6-10) — each is a variation on the theme that the redeemer’s provision exceeds every measure of need.
2.18: A theme: Ruth returns to Naomi carrying not only the gleaned grain but the leftover food from the meal Boaz gave her — she shares what the redeemer gave her. The NT’s economic ethic is built on this pattern: those who have received grace share it (Acts 4:32-35). The overflow of the redeemer’s provision is not hoarded but distributed through the community of the redeemed.
2.19: A theme: Naomi blesses the unknown man before she knows it is Boaz. The recognition sequence — experience grace, then discover the source — mirrors conversion: people encounter the grace of God before they know its name. The Emmaus disciples recognize Christ in the breaking of bread (Luke 24:30-31) after he has already opened the Scriptures to them. The revelation of the redeemer’s name follows the experience of his grace.
2.21: A theme: Boaz’s invitation covers the whole harvest season — not a one-day provision but sustained belonging through the full duration of the work. Christ’s provision is similarly durable: “I am with you always, to the end of the age” (Matt 28:20); “he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion” (Phil 1:6). The grace that begins the harvest does not abandon at midpoint.
2.22: A theme: Naomi’s counsel — stay in Boaz’s field, do not go to another — is the wisdom of proximity to the redeemer. Those who drift from the community of the redeemed expose themselves to harm. The NT’s warnings about drifting (Heb 2:1; 10:25; 1 John 2:19) carry the same structural logic: remain close to the one who provides shelter; do not wander into fields where no one protects you.
2.23: A theme: Ruth gleans through both the barley and wheat harvests — the full grain season, from Passover to Pentecost. The Spirit is poured out at Pentecost (the wheat harvest feast), producing the harvest of the first converts of the new covenant community (Acts 2). Ruth’s endurance through both harvests is a quiet figure for the covenant community that endures from the Passover of the cross to the Pentecost of the Spirit.

1.1: A theme: “In the days when the judges ruled Israel, there was a famine in the land.” The narrative begins with hunger inside the promised land — covenant curse enacted (Deut 28:23-24). The famine that drives Elimelech’s family to Moab is the same pattern as Jacob’s family driven to Egypt: the covenant people in extremity, the inheritance temporarily inaccessible. Christ enters a world under covenant curse — “the whole creation has been groaning” (Rom 8:22) — as the one who will turn famine into fullness.
1.2: A theme: Elimelech (“my God is king”) leaves Bethlehem (“house of bread”) because there is no bread. His sons are Mahlon (“sickness”) and Chilion (“destruction”). The names read as a theological commentary: the one who confesses God as king departs from the house of bread, his sons named for the consequences of covenant crisis. The gospel reversal is total: the true King returns to Bethlehem (Matt 2:1), the house of bread becomes the birthplace of the Bread of Life (John 6:35).
1.3: A theme: Elimelech dies, leaving Naomi widowed in a foreign land. Every death in Ruth contributes to the void the kinsman-redeemer must fill. Christ is the one who abolishes death (2 Tim 1:10) and promises that the bereaved will be comforted (Matt 5:4) — not as cold theology but as the reversal of precisely this kind of irreversible loss.
1.4: A theme: the sons take Moabite wives. This marriage across ethnic lines would have been scandalous (Deut 23:3 excluded Moabites). Yet Matthew and Luke both name Ruth explicitly in the genealogy of Christ (Matt 1:5; Luke 3:32). The messianic line threads through the supposedly excluded: Tamar, Rahab, Ruth, Bathsheba. The inclusion of the unexpected is not oversight — it is the grammar of grace, establishing that Christ comes for all nations.
1.5: A theme: both sons die — triple bereavement in a foreign land. No male heir, no land, no future by any human calculation. This is the nadir from which only a redeemer can produce restoration. Christ enters the nadir of human covenant failure: “while we were still sinners, Christ died for us” (Rom 5:8). Redemption begins from the lowest point, not after partial recovery.
1.6: A revelation of God: Naomi hears that “the LORD had visited his people and given them food.” The word pāqad (visited) is the Exodus vocabulary of divine attendance to the afflicted (Gen 50:24-25; Exod 3:16). Zechariah’s prophecy at John’s birth uses this exact vocabulary: “Blessed be the Lord God of Israel, for he has visited and redeemed his people” (Luke 1:68). The visitation that brings bread to Bethlehem anticipates the Visitor who becomes the Bread.
1.7: A theme: Naomi sets out to return from Moab to Bethlehem with both daughters-in-law. Ruth’s journey from Moab to Israel’s covenant community is the individual form of what Isaiah prophesied at cosmic scale: the nations streaming to Zion (Isa 2:2-3). The Gentile’s personal covenant entry points toward the great eschatological gathering of all nations.
1.8: A theme: Naomi urges her daughters-in-law to return to their mothers’ houses, praying YHWH would show them hesed as they had shown hesed. She releases them from obligation. Christ similarly does not coerce discipleship: “Will you also go away?” (John 6:67). Those who remain do so not because they must but because, with Peter, they have nowhere else to go that gives life (John 6:68).
1.9: A theme: Naomi asks that YHWH grant them mānôaḥ — a settled resting place — in the house of a new husband. This is what she cannot provide; it requires someone outside her household to supply. The rest that no human circumstances can provide is what Christ promises: “Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest” (Matt 11:28). Naomi’s prayer points beyond itself to the one who is himself the rest of his people.
1.10: A theme: both women refuse to leave — weeping, loyal. Ruth’s persistence through Naomi’s three attempts to send her away (vv. 8, 11, 15) is the persistence of genuine covenant belonging. The NT makes the same point about the security of those who belong to Christ: they cannot ultimately be argued or pressured away (John 10:29: “no one can snatch them out of the Father’s hand”).
1.11: A theme: Naomi’s levirate logic — “Do I still have sons in my womb?” — exposes the limits of human legal provision. The kinsman-redeemer institution requires a living male relative; Naomi has none. The redeemer must come from outside the apparent options. The incarnation similarly comes from beyond what human biological or legal structures can provide: “born of a woman” (Gal 4:4), not from any human father’s initiative.
1.12: A theme: “I am too old to have a husband. Even if I said I had hope...” The repetition of impossibility strengthens the typological pattern: what the law requires, human capacity cannot supply. Sarah’s laughter, Hannah’s barrenness, Elizabeth’s old age — each case sharpens the pattern that climaxes in the virgin birth, where YHWH provides what no human mechanism can produce.
1.13: A revelation of God: “the LORD’s hand has gone out against me.” Naomi reads her own affliction as divine action. The same God who afflicts is the one who can restore — and only YHWH can reverse what YHWH has done. The gospel promises this exact reversal: the one whose law condemns (YHWH’s hand against us) provides the redeemer (YHWH’s own Son for us).
1.14: A shadow: Orpah kisses Naomi and returns; Ruth clings (dābaq) to her. The same covenant-bond verb used for marriage (Gen 2:24) and Israel’s attachment to YHWH (Deut 10:20) governs Ruth’s choice. The two daughters represent the two responses to covenant invitation: returning to the familiar, or clinging to the people and God of the promise. The NT knows the same fork: many disciples turned back (John 6:66) while the Twelve clung.
1.15: A theme: Naomi frames the alternative as a return to “her people and her gods.” The choice is not between Naomi and loneliness; it is between YHWH and Chemosh. False gods do not simply fade — they call back. Christ makes the same exclusive claim: “No one can serve two masters” (Matt 6:24). Ruth’s refusal is a full conversion, not a cultural accommodation.
1.16: A direct type: “Your people shall be my people, and your God my God.” Ruth’s declaration is the OT’s most explicit voluntary covenant-entry by a Gentile. She identifies with the covenant people and their God by an act of personal commitment, not ethnic birth. Paul’s argument that the true children of Abraham are those who share his faith (Rom 4:11-12; Gal 3:7) has its OT paradigm case in Ruth. She is the grammar of grace: Gentile faith-entry into the covenant.
1.17: A shadow: “Where you die, I will die, and there I will be buried.” Ruth’s commitment extends to death and burial — full solidarity with Naomi’s mortality. This death-solidarity anticipates Christ’s solidarity with humanity in death: “since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things, that through death he might destroy the one who has the power of death” (Heb 2:14). The redeemer must enter death to conquer it.
1.18: A theme: when Naomi saw Ruth was mit’ammeṣet — firmly resolved — she stopped arguing. The resolve of genuine faith is not shaken by rational objections. Those who belong to Christ are similarly described as those who cannot be snatched from the Father’s hand (John 10:29) — held not by their own grip but by the Spirit’s unbreakable work in them.
1.19: A theme: they arrive in Bethlehem and the whole city is stirred. Bethlehem — house of bread — is the place where bread returns: first in the harvest, then in Boaz’s son, and ultimately in the birth of the Bread of Life (John 6:51). Providence has directed the journey to exactly the right city, as it will when a census brings Mary and Joseph to the same place eight centuries later (Luke 2:4-7).
1.20: A theme: Naomi asks to be called Mara (“bitter”), “for the Almighty has dealt very bitterly with me.” The name-change marks the distance between who she was and what she has become. The gospel reverses name-changes in the other direction: Abram → Abraham, Simon → Peter. Those who come to Christ bearing names given by affliction receive a new name written by the redeemer himself (Rev 2:17; 3:12).
1.21: A revelation of God: “I went out full, and the LORD has brought me back empty.” YHWH’s answer to Naomi’s emptiness is already prepared: the narrative has planted Boaz in position (1:22 brings Ruth to Bethlehem at the barley harvest). YHWH’s response to “I am empty” is already in place before the prayer is voiced (Isa 65:24: “Before they call I will answer”).
1.22: A theme: they came to Bethlehem “at the beginning of the barley harvest.” The arrival at harvest time is the book’s first hint that emptiness will become fullness. Christ uses harvest as his primary image for the gathering of those who belong to him (Matt 9:37-38). The grain that falls and dies bears much fruit (John 12:24). The whole harvest economy Boaz presides over is the economic form of the gospel’s abundance.

3.1: A theme: Naomi seeks mānôaḥ — a settled resting place — for Ruth. The word carries the Sabbath-rest theme that runs from creation (Gen 2:2-3) through the conquest (Josh 21:44) into the prophets’ eschatological promise (Isa 28:12; Jer 6:16). Ruth needs more than gleaning rights; she needs the security of a covenant household. Christ offers exactly this: “Come to me... and I will give you rest” (Matt 11:28) — not the rest of inactivity but the settled security of belonging to the redeemer.
3.2: A shadow: Boaz is winnowing barley at the threshing floor tonight. The threshing floor is the OT’s primary image for judgment and sorting — the separation of grain from chaff. John the Baptist uses exactly this image for Christ’s coming: “his winnowing fork is in his hand, and he will clear his threshing floor and gather his wheat into the barn, but the chaff he will burn with unquenchable fire” (Matt 3:12). The threshing floor where Ruth asks for redemption is the space where the eschatological Redeemer will accomplish final separation.
3.3: A shadow: Naomi’s instructions to Ruth — wash, anoint yourself, put on your best garments — are the preparation of the bride for meeting the one who may redeem her. The same preparation language governs the vision of the new Jerusalem: “the bride has made herself ready... arrayed in fine linen, bright and pure” (Rev 19:7-8). The church comes to Christ prepared by the Spirit’s work, presenting herself in deliberate readiness.
3.4: A shadow: “Uncover his feet and lie down, and he will tell you what to do.” The posture at the redeemer’s feet — vulnerable, waiting for his word — is the posture of those who meet Jesus: Mary sitting at his feet (Luke 10:39), the woman washing his feet with tears (Luke 7:38), the disciples after the resurrection falling at his feet (Matt 28:9). The one who lies at the redeemer’s feet awaits his word; that word will be good news.
3.5: A theme: “I will do everything you say.” Ruth’s complete trust in Naomi’s instruction — even when the instruction is socially risky and its logic unexplained — models the faith that follows the covenant mediator’s direction without demanding prior explanation. The disciples’ immediate response to Jesus’s call (Matt 4:20: “immediately they left their nets and followed”) has the same shape: trust precedes understanding.
3.6: A theme: Ruth goes and does exactly as instructed — complete obedience before the outcome is known. Christ’s own obedience to the Father is the model: “although he was a son, he learned obedience through what he suffered” (Heb 5:8); “not my will, but yours, be done” (Luke 22:42). The pattern of faith is: hear, trust, obey — in that order, without requiring prior knowledge of the result.
3.7: A theme: Boaz’s heart is glad after eating and drinking — the harvest complete, provision made — and he lies down at the far end of the grain heap. The redeemer at rest after the work is complete anticipates Christ who, having accomplished the work the Father gave him, “sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high” (Heb 1:3). The redeemer’s rest after completed work signals not abandonment but the perfection of what was done.
3.8: A theme: at midnight Boaz startles and finds a woman lying at his feet. The midnight encounter — the one who waits in the right posture for the redeemer’s discovery — echoes the parable of the ten virgins: “at midnight there was a cry, Here is the bridegroom!” (Matt 25:6). Those found waiting at the redeemer’s feet are received; those who are not in position at the critical moment find the door shut.
3.9: A type: “Spread your garment over your servant, for you are a kinsman-redeemer.” Ruth takes the wing-imagery of 2:12 — “under whose wings you have come to take refuge” — and asks Boaz to be its physical embodiment. Ezek 16:8 uses the same gesture for YHWH’s covenant with Israel: “I spread the corner of my garment over you and you became mine.” The redeemer who shelters under YHWH’s wings becomes the wing for those who shelter in him. In Christ, YHWH’s protective love takes flesh.
3.10: A theme: Boaz says Ruth’s latter act of hesed is greater than the former — loyalty that perseveres beyond the convenient. Christ honors persistent, exceeding faith: the centurion (“not even in Israel have I found such faith” — Matt 8:10), the Syrophoenician woman’s refusal to accept the first answer (Matt 15:28), the widow’s two coins (Mark 12:44). The hesed that goes beyond what circumstance demands is the form of faith the redeemer most explicitly commends.
3.11: A revelation of God: “All in the gate know you are a woman of worth (ʾēšet ḥayil).” The public community testifies to the outsider’s character — the one who entered as a Moabite is now known by her hesed. Christ names his own publicly before his Father (Matt 10:32: “I will also acknowledge before my Father who is in heaven”). The redeemer endorses the character of those who follow him; the community’s witness is the social form of that endorsement.
3.12: A theme: Boaz acknowledges the nearer kinsman honestly. He does not hide the legal complexity or skip the required procedure. The redeemer is fully transparent about what stands between the petition and redemption’s completion. Christ likewise fulfills the law’s requirements completely rather than bypassing them (Matt 5:17: “I have not come to abolish the Law and the Prophets but to fulfill them”).
3.13: A type: “As the LORD lives, I will redeem you.” Boaz’s vow by YHWH’s own life is the ground of certainty for Ruth’s hope. The redeemer’s commitment is secured by the eternal life of God. The writer of Hebrews makes the same argument for Christ’s priesthood: his intercession is permanent because he “holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever” (Heb 7:24). Ruth can rest at the redeemer’s feet because his vow is sworn on the life that cannot end.
3.14: A theme: Boaz sends Ruth away before dawn to protect her reputation. The redeemer guards the honor of the one he is in process of redeeming. Christ’s intercessory work is this same ongoing protection: “he always lives to make intercession for them” (Heb 7:25). The period between the promise of redemption and its completion is not a time of exposure but of active intercession.
3.15: A shadow: six measures of barley given before the transaction closes — the redeemer’s pledge of intention. The earnest payment before full redemption mirrors the Spirit as the “pledge (arrabōn) of our inheritance until we acquire full possession of it” (Eph 1:14; 2 Cor 1:22). The gift before the completion demonstrates that the redeemer’s commitment is real; the Spirit given now guarantees the fullness still to come.
3.16: A theme: Ruth reports everything Boaz said and did. The witness who returns from the redeemer’s presence and tells the full story is the NT disciple’s role: “we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard” (Acts 4:20). Testimony to the redeemer’s words and actions — exact content, specific character — is the form that gospel proclamation takes in every generation.
3.17: A theme: “He gave me these six measures of barley with the instruction not to go back empty-handed.” The redeemer sends provision back to those not yet in his presence. Naomi receives from Ruth what Boaz gave — grace transmitted through the one who has been with the redeemer to those not yet reached. The gospel’s transmission follows this pattern: the community that has met Christ carries his provision to those not yet brought near.
3.18: A theme: “Stay still, my daughter, until you know how the matter will turn out. The man will not rest until the matter is settled today.” The counsel to “stay still” while the redeemer acts is the posture of those who have received the earnest of the Spirit while awaiting the fullness of redemption. “Be still and know that I am God” (Ps 46:10); “we ourselves groan inwardly as we wait eagerly for adoption” (Rom 8:23). Naomi’s confidence in the redeemer’s urgency is confidence that he “will bring it to completion” (Phil 1:6).

4.1: A theme: Boaz goes to the town gate and sits down — the public, legal arena of the ancient city. The redeemer does not act in secret; he makes his move in the most public space available. Christ’s redemption similarly operates in full public view: the crucifixion is a public execution, the resurrection publicly attested by witnesses (Acts 1:3; 1 Cor 15:3-8), the ascension witnessed by the disciples (Acts 1:9). Redemption happens in history, not myth.
4.2: A theme: Boaz assembles ten elders as witnesses — the formal legal number for covenant testimony. The cloud of witnesses serves to validate the transaction legally and to become the community that holds covenant memory. The NT concept of the “great cloud of witnesses” (Heb 12:1) operates in the same pattern: the covenant transaction requires witnesses who become part of the ongoing story of redemption.
4.3: A theme: Boaz discloses the situation fully — Naomi is selling the land of Elimelech. The redeemer does not manipulate the legal process; full disclosure is required for a just transaction. Christ’s atonement similarly operates through complete transparency: the Father knows what the Son is doing (John 10:17-18); the law’s demands are fully met, not circumvented. Grace addresses the problem fully rather than hiding it.
4.4: A theme: the offer must be made to the nearer kinsman first. The legal order is respected. The gospel has a similar order: “to the Jew first and also to the Greek” (Rom 1:16). The covenant priority is not preferential indifference to the Gentiles but the redemptive-historical sequence in which the promise was given. The offer is genuine — made first to those with the prior claim.
4.5: A shadow: Boaz discloses the fuller obligation: acquiring the land means acquiring Ruth to raise up the dead man’s name. The redemption package is more costly than it first appeared. Christ’s redemption similarly includes the full covenant obligation: not just forgiving sins but taking the church as his bride (Eph 5:25-27), restoring the dead man’s name, raising what sin killed. The scope of redemption is always larger than the initial presentation suggests.
4.6: A shadow: the nearer kinsman declines — “I cannot redeem it, lest I impair my own inheritance.” The law’s prior claim cannot be exercised without self-destructive consequences. Paul’s argument in Galatians and Romans reaches the same conclusion: “all who rely on works of the law are under a curse” (Gal 3:10); “what the law could not do, weak as it was through the flesh, God did” (Rom 8:3). The law is not evil — it has a legitimate claim — but it cannot carry the redemption to completion. Grace steps in where law reaches its limit.
4.7: A shadow: the sandal-removal custom legally relinquishes the right of redemption. This is an adaptation of the Deuteronomic levirate refusal (Deut 25:9-10) without the element of shaming. The law’s form shifts under the pressure of grace: the nearer kinsman is not shamed but simply passes the right. The gospel similarly does not shame the law but fulfills it in a way the law itself could not accomplish (Matt 5:17; Rom 3:31).
4.8: A theme: the sandal is drawn off and the legal right transferred to Boaz. The covenant instrument is now in the redeemer’s hands. Christ receives from the Father the authority to redeem: “All authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me” (Matt 28:18); “the Father loves the Son and has given all things into his hand” (John 3:35). The transfer is complete and public.
4.9: A theme: Boaz declares publicly: “You are witnesses today that I have acquired all that was Elimelech’s.” The redeemer’s purchase is proclaimed before the community, which is enlisted as memory-keepers of the transaction. The resurrection proclamation follows the same form: witnesses, public declaration, community holding the testimony (Acts 2:32; 1 Cor 15:3-7). The gospel is not a private transaction but a publicly witnessed event.
4.10: A type: “I have also acquired Ruth the Moabitess... as my wife, to perpetuate the name of the dead on his inheritance.” The full scope of the goel’s work: to restore to the family line what death cut off. Christ’s resurrection victory is described in the same terms: “Death is swallowed up in victory” (1 Cor 15:54); the one who was dead is alive forever (Rev 1:18). The name that sin threatened to cut off is written in the Lamb’s Book of Life (Rev 21:27).
4.11: A theme: the blessing invokes Rachel and Leah — the matriarchs through whom the twelve tribes came — for Ruth the Moabite. The community prays that the Gentile will become like the covenant mothers of Israel. This is the full reversal of her status under Deut 23:3. Paul’s declaration that those in Christ are “Abraham’s offspring, heirs according to promise” (Gal 3:29) is the theological statement of what this blessing embodies in the life of one Gentile woman.
4.12: A shadow: “May your house be like the house of Perez, whom Tamar bore to Judah.” Perez is the ancestor of the Davidic line (4:18) and his story (Gen 38) involves another Gentile woman who secured the covenant line through desperate covenant loyalty. The mention of Perez names the pattern explicitly: YHWH’s covenant line threads through irregular unions and Gentile women to produce the lineage through which Christ comes. None of this is hidden — it is named in the blessing itself.
4.13: A revelation of God: “the LORD enabled her to conceive.” The birth of Obed is YHWH’s gift — Ruth had apparently been barren during her first marriage (no children mentioned). The divinely-opened womb stands in the series of miraculous births: Sarah, Rebekah, Rachel, Hannah, Elizabeth — each providentially opened to produce a covenant-critical child. The series culminates in the virgin birth, where YHWH conceives without any human father. Every divinely-given birth points toward the birth in which God himself is the Father.
4.14: A revelation of God: the women declare “Blessed be the LORD, who has not left you without a kinsman-redeemer today.” The community recognizes the redemption as YHWH’s act accomplished through a human redeemer — naming both the human agent (Boaz) and the divine source (YHWH) in one blessing. At the resurrection, the community declares the same double truth: the one who acted was born of a woman (Gal 4:4); the one who vindicated him was God who raised him from the dead (Acts 2:24). The redeemer is both fully human and divinely commissioned.
4.15: A theme: the kinsman-redeemer “will restore your life and sustain you in your old age.” The provision is not for the crisis only but for the whole arc of life — ongoing sustenance, not just momentary rescue. Christ’s redemption encompasses the whole life: “he who began a good work in you will bring it to completion” (Phil 1:6); “I give them eternal life, and they will never perish” (John 10:28). The gospel is not a moment of rescue followed by abandonment but a sustained covenant relationship.
4.16: A theme: Naomi takes the child against her chest and becomes his nurse — the one who was empty (1:21: “the LORD has brought me back empty”) now holds life. The fullness-from-emptiness reversal is complete. The gospel arc moves in this direction: “Blessed are those who mourn, for they shall be comforted” (Matt 5:4); “you have sorrow now, but I will see you again, and your hearts will rejoice” (John 16:22). The narrative shape of Ruth — loss, journey, gleaning, redemption, child — is the gospel story told in miniature.
4.17: A type: “They named him Obed” — ʿōbēd, “one who serves.” The redeemer’s son is a servant. The Davidic line flowing from this servant will produce the Servant of YHWH (Isa 52:13-53:12), the Son of David who came “not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many” (Mark 10:45). The name Obed anticipates the servant-shape of the messianic mission: the one who comes to reign comes first to serve.
4.18: A theme: the genealogy begins with Perez — pāraṣ, “one who breaks through” (Gen 38:29). The messianic genealogy begins with a breakthrough of life against all expectation. The resurrection is the ultimate breakthrough: Christ breaks through death’s finality. Each generation in the genealogy represents YHWH’s preservation of the covenant line against every threat — a series of breakthroughs that finally culminates in the one who breaks through death itself.
4.19: A theme: Hezron, Ram, Amminadab — each name represents a generation in which YHWH preserved the covenant line through ordinary family faithfulness. The continuity of the line across generations of obscurity is divine faithfulness made biographical. Paul’s argument in Romans 9-11 that YHWH’s covenant with Abraham has never failed (Rom 9:6) has its evidence in exactly these chains of preserved names.
4.20: A theme: Amminadab and Nahshon — Nahshon was the leader of Judah at the tabernacle’s inaugural offering (Num 7:12), the first to bring an offering before YHWH’s dwelling. The genealogy threads through the worship leadership of Israel. The covenant line is not separate from Israel’s sanctuary life; it runs through the ones who stood closest to YHWH’s presence. Christ is both the heir of the line and the fulfillment of the tabernacle — the one in whom God fully dwells (John 1:14; Col 2:9).
4.21: A theme: Salmon fathered Boaz. Matthew’s genealogy identifies Salmon as the father of Boaz “by Rahab” (Matt 1:5) — Rahab the Canaanite who sheltered the spies (Josh 2). Two Gentile women in consecutive generations of the Davidic line: Rahab and Ruth. YHWH’s covenant line deliberately incorporates those with no natural claim — prefiguring the gospel’s reach to every nation. Matthew names both women in the genealogy of Jesus, precisely so the Gentile inclusion cannot be missed.
4.22: A direct revelation: “Obed fathered Jesse, and Jesse fathered David.” Ruth ends where it was always going — the throne of David and, through it, the throne of Christ. The genealogy that closes Ruth is the same genealogy that opens Matthew (1:5-6) and appears in Luke 3:32. Every verse of Ruth participates in the narrative that reaches this terminus: the Gentile widow’s covenant loyalty, the kinsman-redeemer’s costly love, the divine gift of a servant-son — all of it the backstory of the throne that the Son of David inherits eternally (Luke 1:32-33). Ruth is the gospel in miniature.
